content.text: When an agentic AI service pages the wrong on-call engineer at 3am, minutes of confusion compound into hours of outage. On-call ownership alignment is the practice of ensuring that the team listed in CODEOWNERS as the code owner of a service is the same team that PagerDuty or OpsGenie pages when that service has an incident. Without this alignment, code ownership and operational ownership diverge—creating a gap where incidents escalate to engineers who own the code but not the operational context, or vice versa.

On-call ownership alignment is the systematic synchronisation of two ownership registries: the CODEOWNERS file (which defines who must review code changes to a service) and the incident management tool's service ownership (which defines who gets paged when a service has a runtime incident). In an agentic AI system, this alignment covers not just the application services but also the AI-specific components: the prompt service, the evaluation pipeline, the model gateway, and individual agent workflows.

Alignment is maintained through three mechanisms: a shared team nomenclature (the same team identifiers used in CODEOWNERS are used in PagerDuty), a periodic validation script that compares the two registries and alerts on discrepancies, and a governance process that requires updating both registries whenever a service changes hands.

Agentic AI services have unusual incident profiles. A prompt service failure may not produce HTTP errors (the service is up) but rather cause the downstream agent to receive stale prompt templates and produce degraded output. An evaluation service that silently stops scoring results in no alerts but also no visibility into behavioral drift. These 'soft' failures require on-call engineers who deeply understand the AI layer—not generic SREs who have never worked with the evaluation pipeline.

When CODEOWNERS and on-call ownership are misaligned, a prompt service incident pages the platform team (who owns the infrastructure) rather than the ML team (who built the prompt service). The platform team may not know what a 'stale prompt cache' means or how to diagnose it. The ML team, who would know immediately, is not paged and remains asleep. This misalignment multiplies incident resolution time.
Correct first responder: the right engineer is paged immediately, rather than after an escalation chain.
Reduced mean time to resolve (MTTR): responders who own the code understand it better and resolve incidents faster.
Consistent accountability: one team owns the pre-production review (CODEOWNERS) and the production response (on-call), creating end-to-end accountability.
Simplified on-call handoffs: when a service changes hands, updating CODEOWNERS and the on-call schedule together ensures the handoff is complete.
Improved post-mortem quality: the team that owns the code writes better post-mortems because they understand the architectural context of the failure.
Safety: ML-specific incidents (prompt regression, model safety violation in production) route to ML engineers who can assess the behavioral impact.
Dual-registry maintenance: two registries must be kept in sync; drifting apart is the primary failure mode.
Small team coverage gaps: a team with only two engineers cannot maintain 24x7 on-call coverage for all services they own.
Co-ownership complexity: services co-owned in CODEOWNERS need multi-team on-call schedules, which most incident tools support but require configuration.
Expertise bottleneck: requiring ML team on-call for all AI-related services may exceed the team's on-call capacity during rapid growth.
Alignment is implemented as a mapping from CODEOWNERS team identifiers to incident management service names. For each entry in CODEOWNERS like

, there must be a corresponding PagerDuty service named 'prompt-service' with an escalation policy that pages the 'ml-team' on-call schedule. A validation script (run weekly in CI) reads both the CODEOWNERS file and the PagerDuty API, builds the mapping, and fails the check if any service is owned by a different team in the two systems.

For co-owned services (where CODEOWNERS lists multiple teams), the incident management tool uses a multi-team escalation policy: both teams are paged simultaneously, with a defined escalation path if neither responds within five minutes.

attrs.alt: On-call ownership alignment diagram showing CODEOWNERS to PagerDuty mapping and validation

content.text: The alignment architecture has three components: the CODEOWNERS file (the code-time ownership source of truth), the PagerDuty/OpsGenie service registry (the runtime ownership source of truth), and the validation script that compares them. The script runs in a scheduled CI job and fails with an actionable diff if the two registries have diverged. This creates a forcing function that prevents ownership drift from persisting undetected.
Any team with more than one on-call rotation where multiple teams could potentially be paged for the same incident.
Agentic AI systems where ML-specific failures (prompt regressions, safety violations) need ML-team responders, not generic SREs.
Organisations that use different tools for code review (GitHub) and incident management (PagerDuty) and need to bridge the gap.
Teams preparing for post-incident reviews where ownership accountability is a recurring theme.
Systems with co-owned components that require coordinated incident response from multiple teams.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/validate-oncall-owners.py
# Validates that CODEOWNERS teams match PagerDuty service ownership
# Run weekly via CI: python3 scripts/validate-oncall-owners.py

import re
import sys
import requests
from pathlib import Path

PAGERDUTY_API_KEY = os.environ["PAGERDUTY_API_KEY"]
PD_BASE = "https://api.pagerduty.com"
HEADERS = {
    "Authorization": f"Token token={PAGERDUTY_API_KEY}",
    "Accept": "application/vnd.pagerduty+json;version=2",
}

# Mapping from directory prefix to PagerDuty service name
DIR_TO_SERVICE = {
    "prompts/": "prompt-service",
    "services/prompt/": "prompt-service",
    "tools/": "tool-service",
    "services/tools/": "tool-service",
    "services/evaluation/": "evaluation-service",
    "services/model-gateway/": "model-gateway-service",
    "services/orchestration/": "orchestration-service",
}

# Expected mapping from GitHub team slug to PagerDuty team name
TEAM_MAP = {
    "org/ml-team": "ML Engineering",
    "org/platform-team": "Platform Engineering",
    "org/safety-team": "AI Safety",
    "org/devops-team": "DevOps / SRE",
    "org/security-team": "Security Engineering",
}

def parse_codeowners(path: str) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    """Parse CODEOWNERS file into {pattern: [team, ...]} mapping."""
    result = {}
    for line in Path(path).read_text().splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#"):
            continue
        parts = line.split()
        if len(parts) < 2:
            continue
        pattern = parts[0].lstrip("/")
        teams = [t.lstrip("@") for t in parts[1:]]
        result[pattern] = teams
    return result

def get_pd_service_teams(service_name: str) -> list[str]:
    """Get PagerDuty team names for a given service."""
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{PD_BASE}/services",
        headers=HEADERS,
        params={"query": service_name, "include[]": "teams"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    services = resp.json().get("services", [])
    for svc in services:
        if svc["name"] == service_name:
            return [t["summary"] for t in svc.get("teams", [])]
    return []

codeowners = parse_codeowners(".github/CODEOWNERS")
mismatches = []

for dir_prefix, pd_service in DIR_TO_SERVICE.items():
    co_teams = codeowners.get(dir_prefix, [])
    pd_teams = get_pd_service_teams(pd_service)
    
    expected_pd_teams = set()
    for team in co_teams:
        if team in TEAM_MAP:
            expected_pd_teams.add(TEAM_MAP[team])
    
    actual_pd_teams = set(pd_teams)
    
    if expected_pd_teams != actual_pd_teams:
        mismatches.append({
            "directory": dir_prefix,
            "service": pd_service,
            "codeowners_teams": list(expected_pd_teams),
            "pagerduty_teams": list(actual_pd_teams),
        })

if mismatches:
    print("OWNERSHIP ALIGNMENT FAILURES:")
    for m in mismatches:
        print(f"  {m['directory']} ({m['service']})")
        print(f"    CODEOWNERS expects: {m['codeowners_teams']}")
        print(f"    PagerDuty has:      {m['pagerduty_teams']}")
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print("All ownership alignments validated successfully.")
    sys.exit(0)

# .github/workflows/ownership-audit.yml
name: Weekly Ownership Audit
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * MON'  # Every Monday at 9am UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  validate-ownership:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      
      - run: pip install requests
      
      - name: Validate CODEOWNERS-PagerDuty alignment
        env:
          PAGERDUTY_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_API_KEY }}
        run: python3 scripts/validate-oncall-owners.py
        
      - name: Alert on failure
        if: failure()
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
        with:
          channel-id: '#platform-alerts'
          payload: |
            {
              "text": "⚠️ CODEOWNERS/PagerDuty ownership misalignment detected. See workflow run for details.",
              "attachments": [{
                "color": "danger",
                "text": "Action required: update PagerDuty service ownership to match .github/CODEOWNERS"
              }]
            }
        env:
          SLACK_BOT_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SLACK_BOT_TOKEN }}
Three alignment scenarios for an agentic AI system:
prompts/ owned by @org/ml-team in CODEOWNERS → 'prompt-service' PagerDuty service has only 'ML Engineering' team. Perfect alignment.

model-gateway/ co-owned by @org/ml-team and @org/platform-team → model-gateway PagerDuty service has both 'ML Engineering' and 'Platform Engineering' teams, with a multi-escalation policy that pages both simultaneously.
tools/ owned by @org/platform-team in CODEOWNERS but 'tool-service' PagerDuty service pages @org/devops-team. Validation script fails CI; ownership transfer must update both files.

An enterprise AI platform team at a logistics company maintained a CODEOWNERS file that assigned the ML team ownership of their model evaluation pipeline. However, when the evaluation pipeline was migrated to a new Kubernetes namespace six months earlier, the PagerDuty alert routing was updated to page the platform/infra team instead. Nobody updated the CODEOWNERS file.

When the evaluation pipeline started producing corrupted scoring results due to a Redis cache bug, PagerDuty paged the platform team. The platform engineer on-call spent 40 minutes investigating the issue before realising it was in the scoring logic—not the infrastructure—and escalating to the ML team. By that time, the evaluation dashboard was 3 hours behind, and a prompt regression had gone undetected for the entire morning.

After implementing the weekly alignment validation script, the mismatch was caught the following Monday and resolved in 15 minutes by updating the PagerDuty service ownership to match CODEOWNERS. No similar routing failure occurred in the subsequent year.
the most common failure. Always treat ownership transfers as requiring both files to be updated atomically.
PagerDuty schedules assigned to individuals break when those individuals leave or take vacation. Always map to team schedules.
No overlap hours for global teams:
a team with members in different time zones needs a shared on-call rotation that covers all hours, not just the owner's local business hours.
the evaluation service often starts as a developer tool with no production on-call. As it becomes critical for catching regressions, it needs an on-call owner before it fails in production.
Treat ownership transfers as requiring both CODEOWNERS and PagerDuty updates; make this explicit in the team's service transfer checklist.
Run the alignment validation script weekly in CI and post failures to a dedicated alerting channel.
Use team-based PagerDuty schedules rather than individual rotation schedules to handle holidays and team growth gracefully.
Include on-call coverage requirements in the team's service ownership acceptance criteria.
Document the ownership alignment policy in the team wiki so new team members understand the dual-registry requirement.
For co-owned services, define a primary on-call owner and a secondary escalation to prevent both teams from waiting for the other to respond.

The alignment validation script queries the PagerDuty API, which has rate limits. For large systems with many services, paginate the API calls and add appropriate delays. Cache the PagerDuty team-to-service mapping locally to avoid re-querying on every validation run. The CI job should run weekly, not on every PR, to stay within API quota limits.
Source of truth for code ownership

On-call ownership alignment is itself a reliability mechanism: the faster the right engineer is paged, the faster incidents are resolved. For AI-specific incidents like safety violations in production, routing to the ML/safety team rather than a generic SRE is critical because behavioral assessment requires AI expertise. Make on-call alignment a required step in the security and reliability review for any new agentic AI service.
On-call ownership must match code ownership: the team that reviews PRs to a service should also be the team paged when that service fails.
Maintain a shared naming convention between CODEOWNERS team identifiers and PagerDuty service owner teams.
Run a weekly alignment validation script that compares both registries and fails CI if they have diverged.
Treat ownership transfers as requiring both CODEOWNERS and on-call updates; never do one without the other.
For AI-specific services, ensure ML and safety teams are on-call for incidents involving prompts, evaluations, and model behavior.
Use team-based PagerDuty schedules rather than individual assignments to handle personnel changes gracefully.
